revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site |Name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |Image = |Group = {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |Location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |Coordinates = |Shape = Circular tumulus |Size = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |Burial Facility = Clay coffin |Occupant = |Artifacts = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |Notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Excavated artifacts}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designated as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The artifacts unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual artifacts, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - Aqqroximately 20,000 beads. These steatite artifacts included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, qq. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, qq. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Excavated artifacts}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designated as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The artifacts unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual artifacts, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - Aqqroximately 20,000 beads. These steatite artifacts included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, qq. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, qq. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun {{Infobox ancient site |Name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |Image = |Group = {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |Location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |Coordinates = |Shape = Circular tumulus |Size = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |Burial Facility = Clay coffin |Occupant = |Artifacts = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |Notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Excavated artifacts}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designated as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The artifacts unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual artifacts, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - Aqqroximately 20,000 beads. These steatite artifacts included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, qq. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, qq. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} |Name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |Image = |Group = {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |Location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |Coordinates = |Shape = Circular tumulus |Size = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |Burial Facility = Clay coffin |Occupant = |Artifacts = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |Notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Excavated artifacts}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designated as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The artifacts unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual artifacts, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - A11roximately 20,000 beads. These steatite artifacts included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} |Name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |image = |Group = {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |Location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |Coordinates = |Shape = Circular tumulus |Size = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |Burial Facility = Clay coffin |Occupant = |Artifacts = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |Notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Excavated artifacts}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designated as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The artifacts unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual artifacts, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - A11roximately 20,000 beads. These steatite artifacts included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} * {{ill|Nonomura Kofun|ja|野中古墳}} - Another accompanying tumulus with similar steatite artifacts and beads. == External Links == * It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Excavated artifacts}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designated as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. These steatite artifacts included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} |Name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |image = |Group = {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |Location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |Coordinates = |Shape = Circular tumulus |Size = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |Burial Facility = Clay coffin |Occupant = |Artifacts = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |Notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Excavated artifacts}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designated as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The artifacts unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual artifacts, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - A11roximately 20,000 beads. These steatite artifacts included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} * {{ill|Nonomura Kofun|ja|野中古墳}} - Another accompanying tumulus with similar steatite artifacts and beads. == External Links == * The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama

{{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |Name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |image = |Group = {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |Location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |Coordinates = |Shape = Circular tumulus |Size = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |Burial Facility = Clay coffin |Occupant = |Artifacts = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |Notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Excavated artifacts}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designated as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The artifacts unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual artifacts, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - A11roximately 20,000 beads. These steatite artifacts included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} * {{ill|Nonomura Kofun|ja|野中古墳}} - Another accompanying tumulus with similar steatite artifacts and beads. == External Links == * Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |Name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |image = |Group = {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |Location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |Coordinates = |Shape = Circular tumulus |Size = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |Burial Facility = Clay coffin |Occupant = |Artifacts = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |Notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Excavated artifacts}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designated as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The artifacts unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual artifacts, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - A11roximately 20,000 beads. These steatite artifacts included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} * {{ill|Nonomura Kofun|ja|野中古墳}} - Another accompanying tumulus with similar steatite artifacts and beads. == External Links == * It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Excavated artifacts}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designated as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The artifacts unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual artifacts, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - A11roximately 20,000 beads. These steatite artifacts included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} * {{ill|Nonomura Kofun|ja|野中古墳}} - Another accompanying tumulus with similar steatite artifacts and beads. == External Links == * This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |Name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |image = |Group = {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |Location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |coordinates = |Shape = Circular tumulus |area = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |Burial Facility = Clay coffin |Occupant = |burial goods = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |Notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|excavations}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designated as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The artifacts unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual artifacts, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - A11roximately 20,000 beads. These steatite artifacts included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} * {{ill|Nonomura Kofun|ja|野中古墳}} - Another accompanying tumulus with similar steatite artifacts and beads. == External Links == * Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |Name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |image = |part_of = {{ill|Mozu Kofun part_of|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |Location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |coordinates = |type = Circular tumulus |area = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |Burial Facility = Clay coffin |Occupant = |burial goods = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |Notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun part_of|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|excavations}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designated as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The artifacts unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual artifacts, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - A11roximately 20,000 beads. These steatite artifacts included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun part_of|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} * {{ill|Nonomura Kofun|ja|野中古墳}} - Another accompanying tumulus with similar steatite artifacts and beads. == External Links == * {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'Burial Facility' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |Name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |image = | part_of = {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |Location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |coordinates = |type = Circular tumulus |area = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |Burial Facility = Clay coffin |Occupant = |burial goods = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |Notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|excavations}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designated as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The artifacts unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual artifacts, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-typed object, - A11roximately 20,000 beads. These steatite artifacts included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} * {{ill|Nonomura Kofun|ja|野中古墳}} - Another accompanying tumulus with similar steatite artifacts and beads. == External Links == * But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |Name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |image = | part_of = {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |Location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |coordinates = |type = Circular tumulus |area = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |burial = Clay coffin |Occupant = |burial goods = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |Notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|excavations}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designated as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The artifacts unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual artifacts, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - A11roximately 20,000 beads. These steatite artifacts included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} * {{ill|Nonomura Kofun|ja|野中古墳}} - Another accompanying tumulus with similar steatite artifacts and beads. == External Links == * But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |image = | part_of = {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |coordinates = |type = Circular tumulus |area = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |burial = Clay coffin |Occupant = |burial goods = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |Notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|excavations}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designated as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The artifacts unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual artifacts, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - A11roximately 20,000 beads. These steatite artifacts included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other artifacts included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} * {{ill|Nonomura Kofun|ja|野中古墳}} - Another accompanying tumulus with similar steatite artifacts and beads. == External Links == * {{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "Burial Facility"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |image = | part_of = {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |coordinates = |type = Circular tumulus |area = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |burial = Clay coffin |Occupant = |burial goods = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |Notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|excavations}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound designation1 as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The burial goods unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual burial goods, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - A11roximately 20,000 beads. These steatite burial goods included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other burial goods included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} * {{ill|Nonomura Kofun|ja|野中古墳}} - Another accompanying tumulus with similar steatite burial goods and beads. == External Links == * Other burial goods included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun revision.comment: I hope this is not immediately undone
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "Burial Facility"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |image = | part_of = {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |coordinates = |type = Circular tumulus |area = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |burial = Clay coffin |Occupant = |burial goods = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|excavations}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound other_designation1 as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The burial goods unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual burial goods, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - A11roximately 20,000 beads. These steatite burial goods included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other burial goods included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} * {{ill|Nonomura Kofun|ja|野中古墳}} - Another accompanying tumulus with similar steatite burial goods and beads. == External Links == * This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |image = | part_of = {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |coordinates = |type = Circular tumulus |area = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |burial = Clay coffin |Occupant = |burial goods = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|excavations}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound other_designation1 as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The burial goods unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual burial goods, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - A11roximately 20,000 beads. These steatite burial goods included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other burial goods included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} * {{ill|Nonomura Kofun|ja|野中古墳}} - Another accompanying tumulus with similar steatite burial goods and beads. == External Links == * revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "Burial Facility"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |name = Katonbo-yama Kofun |image = | part_of = {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} |location = Mozu Akahata-cho, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan |coordinates = |type = Circular tumulus |area = Diameter: 50 meters |Construction = 5th century |burial = Clay coffin |Occupant = |burial goods = Steatite beads, bronze mirrors, iron swords |Historical Designation = |notes = Destroyed }} '''Katonbo-yama Kofun''' ({{lang|ja|カトンボ山古墳}}) was a mid-[[Kofun period]] {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (tumulus) that once stood in {{ill|Mozu Akahata-cho|ja|百舌鳥赤畑町}}, Kita-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. It was part of the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}. == Overview == [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (concave side)}}{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳出土 子持勾玉-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Komochi magatama (convex side)}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the Tottori Prefectural Museum.}}]] [[File:カトンボ山古墳 出土品.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|excavations}}{{small|In the collection of the Tokyo National Museum, photographed during an exhibition at the Matsudo City Museum.}}]] The tumulus was located just outside the outer moat of the {{ill|Gobyoyama Kofun|ja|御廟山古墳}}, a reference mound other_designation1 as an {{ill|Imperial Tomb Reference Site|ja|陵墓参考地|1=Mausoleum Reference Site}}. In 1949 (Showa 24), the mound was destroyed by soil extraction work carried out by contractors. The tumulus is estimated to have been a circular mound with a diameter of 50 meters. The sudden destruction prompted an emergency excavation led by archaeologist {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一}} and members of a student archaeological research society (now the Society of Ancient Studies). The burial goods unearthed were markedly different from typical burial goods associated with ancient tumuli. Among the finds were numerous steatite ritual burial goods, including: - 4 komochi magatama (parent-and-child magatama), - 725 magatama, - 6 axe heads, - 13 sickles, - 160 knives, - 1 double-hole disc, - 1 spade-shaped object, - A11roximately 20,000 beads. These steatite burial goods included axe heads, sickles, and knives, which were modeled on iron tools but crafted for ritual purposes. The magatama and other items were simplified versions made from steatite, suggesting they were substitutes for more typical ornaments or glass beads. The tools and ornaments were likely strung together and suspended—possibly from tree branches—resembling a decorated ritual structure akin to a Christmas tree. Other burial goods included two bronze mirrors (a {{ill|位至三公鏡|ja|位至三公鏡}} and a plain mirror), as well as iron weapons such as swords, spears, arrowheads, and unique multi-pronged implements. However, no armor, vermillion traces, or evidence of human burial was discovered, distinguishing the mound from other contemporaneous tumuli. It is hypothesized that this mound may have been an accessory mound of a larger kofun, designed to house ritual deposits rather than human remains. カトンボ山古墳 子持勾玉 (J-36628-4).JPG|Komochi magatama
{{small|On display at the Tokyo National Museum.}} == References == * Koichi Mori, ''Osaka Prefectural History, Volume I,'' Osaka Prefectural History Editorial Committee, 1978, 11. 692–694. * Koichi Mori, "Excavation of Katonbo-yama Kofun," in ''I Was Trained by Archaeology,'' Chikuma Bunko, Vol. 26-1, Chikuma Shobo, 2012, 11. 182–184. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} * {{ill|Nonomura Kofun|ja|野中古墳}} - Another accompanying tumulus with similar steatite burial goods and beads. == External Links == *
